answer:
Definition

An API Gateway is a single entry point that sits between clients and backend microservices. It handles routing, authentication, rate limiting, request/response transformation, and aggregation — so individual services don't need to.

Concrete Use-Case

In an e-commerce app, the mobile client calls GET /api/products/featured. The gateway authenticates the JWT, checks the rate limit, then fans out to the Product Service, Pricing Service, and Review Service in parallel, merges the responses, and returns a single payload. The client never knows three services were involved.

Architecture Flow

Code Example

// Express.js API Gateway (simplified)
const express = require('express');
const { createProxyMiddleware } = require('http-proxy-middleware');

const app = express();

// Auth middleware
app.use((req, res, next) => {
  const token = req.headers.authorization?.split(' ')[1];
  if (!token) return res.status(401).json({ error: 'No token' });
  try {
    req.user = jwt.verify(token, process.env.JWT_SECRET);
    next();
  } catch { res.status(403).json({ error: 'Invalid token' }); }
});

// Route to microservices
app.use('/api/products', createProxyMiddleware({
  target: 'http://product-service:3001',
  pathRewrite: { '^/api/products': '/products' }
}));

app.use('/api/orders', createProxyMiddleware({
  target: 'http://order-service:3002'
}));

app.listen(3000);

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: The gateway becomes a single point of failure. If it goes down, every service is unreachable.

Mitigation: Run multiple gateway instances behind a load balancer. Use health checks, circuit breakers (e.g., opossum library), and keep the gateway stateless so any instance can handle any request.

Validation

Dev: Write integration tests that hit the gateway and assert correct routing + auth rejection. Prod: Monitor gateway latency via percentile dashboards (p50/p95/p99) and set alerts on error rate spikes.

Definition

An API Gateway solves the client-to-microservice coupling problem. Without it, the client must know the address and protocol of every service, handle auth tokens per service, and deal with CORS, versioning, and retries independently.

Concrete Use-Case

Our team had a React dashboard calling 5 different backend services directly. Each had its own auth check, rate limit logic, and error format. Users saw inconsistent error messages and the mobile app broke when we moved the User Service to a new port. After introducing an API Gateway, the client hit one URL, and the gateway handled routing, unified error formatting, and token validation centrally.

Problems Solved

  • Service discovery: Clients don't need to know internal service URLs.
  • Cross-cutting concerns: Auth, logging, rate limiting live in one place instead of duplicated across services.
  • Protocol translation: Gateway can expose REST to clients while calling gRPC services internally.
  • Response aggregation: Combine data from multiple services into one response, reducing client round-trips.

Architecture Flow

Debugging Narrative

We had intermittent 502s in production. Without the gateway's centralized logging, we would have checked each service individually. Instead, the gateway access logs showed that the Order Service was timing out under load. We added a 3-second timeout with a circuit breaker, and 502s dropped to zero.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Putting business logic in the gateway (e.g., data validation, transformations). This creates tight coupling and makes the gateway hard to maintain.

Mitigation: Keep the gateway thin — only routing, auth, rate limiting, and logging. All business logic stays in the services.

Assumptions

  • Mid-size SaaS product with ~50K MAU
  • 3-5 backend services (User, Product, Order, Notification)
  • React frontend + React Native mobile app
  • Team of 4-6 developers

Architecture Design

I'd use Express.js + http-proxy-middleware for the gateway rather than a full solution like Kong or AWS API Gateway. For a mid-size product, a custom lightweight gateway gives us full control without vendor lock-in or operational overhead.

API Contracts

// Route configuration
const routes = {
  '/api/users':    { target: 'http://user-svc:3001',    rateLimit: 100 },
  '/api/products': { target: 'http://product-svc:3002',  rateLimit: 200 },
  '/api/orders':   { target: 'http://order-svc:3003',    rateLimit: 50  },
  '/api/notify':   { target: 'http://notif-svc:3004',    rateLimit: 30  },
};

Object.entries(routes).forEach(([path, config]) => {
  app.use(path,
    rateLimit({ windowMs: 60000, max: config.rateLimit }),
    createProxyMiddleware({ target: config.target, changeOrigin: true })
  );
});

Observability

Add morgan for access logs, prom-client for metrics (request count, latency histogram), and correlation IDs via x-request-id header propagated to all services.

Alternative Considered

I considered AWS API Gateway + Lambda but rejected it because: (1) cold starts add 200-500ms latency, (2) vendor lock-in makes local development harder, (3) for 50K MAU the cost of a managed gateway isn't justified when a simple Express proxy works.

Testing Strategy

Integration tests: spin up gateway + mock services with nock, verify routing, auth rejection, and rate limiting. Load test with autocannon to verify the gateway handles 500 req/s without degradation.

Debugging Scenario: API Gateway Returning 504 Timeout

Step 1 — Reproduce & Gather Context

Users report intermittent slow responses. I check the gateway access logs:

// Gateway logs show:
2024-03-15 14:23:01 POST /api/orders 504 30012ms
2024-03-15 14:23:05 POST /api/orders 504 30008ms
2024-03-15 14:23:02 GET  /api/products 200 45ms   ← other routes fine

Only /api/orders is affected. Other routes are healthy.

Step 2 — Narrow the Scope

I bypass the gateway and call the Order Service directly:

curl -w '%{time_total}' http://order-service:3002/orders
# Response: 28.5 seconds — the service itself is slow

This confirms the issue is in the Order Service, not the gateway routing.

Step 3 — Root Cause

Checking the Order Service logs reveals an N+1 query problem — each order fetches customer details individually:

// BAD: N+1 query
const orders = await Order.findAll();
for (const order of orders) {
  order.customer = await Customer.findById(order.customerId); // 💥 1 query per order
}

// FIX: Eager loading
const orders = await Order.findAll({
  include: [{ model: Customer, as: 'customer' }]
});

Step 4 — Gateway-Level Mitigation

While fixing the root cause, I also add a timeout + circuit breaker at the gateway level:

const CircuitBreaker = require('opossum');

const orderBreaker = new CircuitBreaker(callOrderService, {
  timeout: 5000,        // 5s max
  errorThresholdPercentage: 50,
  resetTimeout: 10000   // retry after 10s
});

orderBreaker.fallback(() => ({
  error: 'Order service temporarily unavailable',
  retryAfter: 10
}));

Step 5 — Verify

After deploying the fix: p95 latency for /api/orders dropped from 28s to 120ms. Circuit breaker prevents cascading failures during future incidents.

Pitfall

Don't just increase the gateway timeout. That masks the real problem and makes the gateway hold connections longer, eventually exhausting the connection pool under load.

Constraints & Requirements

  • 1M+ monthly active users, ~10K concurrent requests
  • 99.9% uptime SLO (max 43 min downtime/month)
  • p99 latency < 200ms at the gateway layer
  • Multi-region deployment for geographic redundancy

Architecture

Key Decisions

DecisionChoiceRationale
Gateway runtimeNGINX + Lua / KongC-level performance, 50K+ req/s per instance
Rate limitingRedis sliding windowDistributed, shared across gateway instances
AuthJWT with short TTL + refreshStateless verification, no DB call per request
Service discoveryKubernetes DNSNative, no extra infrastructure
ConfigDeclarative YAML + GitOpsVersion-controlled, auditable changes

Scale & Reliability

  • Horizontal scaling: Auto-scale gateway pods based on CPU/request rate via HPA
  • Circuit breakers: Per-service circuit breakers with 5s timeout, 50% error threshold
  • Canary deploys: Route 5% traffic to new gateway version, monitor error rates, promote or rollback
  • Caching: Cache GET responses for 30s at gateway level (Redis), reducing backend load by ~60%

Cost Analysis

At 1M MAU (~300M requests/month): 4 gateway pods (2 per region) × $50/month = $200/month vs. AWS API Gateway at $1,050/month (300M × $3.50/M). Custom gateway saves ~$800/month and gives full control.

Migration Strategy

  1. Deploy new gateway alongside existing setup (shadow mode, no traffic)
  2. Mirror 1% of production traffic, compare response codes and latency
  3. Shift 10% → 50% → 100% over 2 weeks using weighted DNS
  4. Keep old gateway on standby for 1 week, then decommission

Common Performance Bottlenecks

BottleneckSymptomRoot CauseResolution
Connection pool exhaustionSudden spike in 503sSlow downstream service holds connectionsSet timeouts + circuit breakers
TLS handshake overheadHigh p99 latency on first requestFull TLS negotiation per connectionEnable connection keep-alive + TLS session resumption
Large payload serializationHigh CPU on gateway podsJSON.stringify on large response bodiesStream responses instead of buffering; use compression
Regex-based routingLatency increases with route countO(n) regex matching on every requestUse trie-based router (e.g., find-my-way)

Instrumentation Strategy

// Prometheus metrics for gateway
const client = require('prom-client');

const httpDuration = new client.Histogram({
  name: 'gateway_http_duration_seconds',
  help: 'Duration of HTTP requests through the gateway',
  labelNames: ['method', 'route', 'status_code', 'target_service'],
  buckets: [0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2.5, 5]
});

const activeConnections = new client.Gauge({
  name: 'gateway_active_connections',
  help: 'Number of active connections to downstream services',
  labelNames: ['target_service']
});

// Middleware to track metrics
app.use((req, res, next) => {
  const end = httpDuration.startTimer();
  res.on('finish', () => {
    end({ method: req.method, route: req.route?.path || req.path,
          status_code: res.statusCode, target_service: req.targetService });
  });
  next();
});

Debugging Narrative

In production, our gateway p99 jumped from 50ms to 2.5s during peak hours. Grafana showed the gateway_active_connections gauge maxing out at 100 (our pool limit) for the Payment Service.

Investigation: The Payment Service had a downstream dependency (Stripe API) that occasionally took 8-10s. Our gateway held connections open waiting for the response, exhausting the pool.

Fix (3-layer approach):

  1. Timeout: Set 5s proxy timeout for the Payment route
  2. Circuit breaker: Open circuit after 3 consecutive timeouts, return cached "processing" response
  3. Async pattern: Refactored to accept payment request immediately (202 Accepted), process asynchronously, notify via webhook

Result: p99 dropped to 80ms. Connection pool utilization stayed under 40% even during peak.

Monitoring Checklist

  • Dashboard: Request rate, error rate, latency percentiles (p50/p95/p99) per route
  • Alert: Error rate > 1% for 5 minutes → page on-call
  • Alert: p99 latency > 500ms for 10 minutes → Slack notification
  • Alert: Connection pool > 80% utilized → auto-scale trigger
